Code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# First, let's understand the date range and structure
print("Date range:")
print(f"Min date: {df['date'].min()}")
print(f"Max date: {df['date'].max()}")
print(f"\nDate distribution sample:")
date_counts = df['date'].value_counts()
print(f"Min records per date: {date_counts.min()}")
print(f"Max records per date: {date_counts.max()}")
print(f"Mean records per date: {date_counts.mean():.0f}")

# Check date frequency
df_dates = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
print(f"\nDate range (datetime): {df_dates.min()} to {df_dates.max()}")
print(f"Unique dates: {df_dates.nunique()}")
print(f"Days in range: {(df_dates.max() - df_dates.min()).days + 1}")

# Investigate the "Unknown" county issue and data quality
print("County value checks:")
print(f"Counties with 'Unknown': {df[df['county'] == 'Unknown'].shape[0]}")
print(f"\nStates where county='Unknown':")
print(df[df['county'] == 'Unknown']['state'].value_counts().head(10))

print("\n\nFIPS null investigation:")
print(f"FIPS null count: {df['fips'].isna().sum()}")
print(f"FIPS nulls by state:")
df_null_fips = df[df['fips'].isna()]
print(df_null_fips['state'].value_counts().head(10))

print("\n\nFirst 10 rows with county='Washington' (the top county):")
print(df[df['county'] == 'Washington'].head(10))

# Check the state column more carefully - the values seem unusual
print("State distribution:")
print(f"Total unique states: {df['state'].nunique()}")
print(f"All states in dataset:")
states = df['state'].unique()
states_sorted = sorted(states)
print(states_sorted)

# Verify these are actual US states/territories
us_states = ['Alabama', 'Alaska', 'Arizona', 'Arkansas', 'California', 'Colorado', 
             'Connecticut', 'Delaware', 'Florida', 'Georgia', 'Hawaii', 'Idaho', 
             'Illinois', 'Indiana', 'Iowa', 'Kansas', 'Kentucky', 'Louisiana', 
             'Maine', 'Maryland', 'Massachusetts', 'Michigan', 'Minnesota', 
             'Mississippi', 'Missouri', 'Montana', 'Nebraska', 'Nevada', 
             'New Hampshire', 'New Jersey', 'New Mexico', 'New York', 'North Carolina', 
             'North Dakota', 'Ohio', 'Oklahoma', 'Oregon', 'Pennsylvania', 
             'Rhode Island', 'South Carolina', 'South Dakota', 'Tennessee', 'Texas', 
             'Utah', 'Vermont', 'Virginia', 'Washington', 'West Virginia', 'Wisconsin', 
             'Wyoming', 'District of Columbia', 'Puerto Rico', 'Guam', 'Northern Mariana Islands',

non_standard = [s for s in states_sorted if s not in us_states]
print(f"\nNon-standard state values: {non_standard}")

Purpose: Examine FIPS values for validity
Code:
# Re-examine FIPS - the nulls should be checked more carefully
print("FIPS analysis:")
print(f"FIPS dtype: {df['fips'].dtype}")
print(f"FIPS null count: {df['fips'].isna().sum()}")
print(f"FIPS sample values:")
print(df['fips'].dropna().head(20).tolist())

# Check if FIPS values look valid (should be 5-digit numbers)
fips_valid = df['fips'].dropna().apply(lambda x: 1000 <= x <= 79999)
print(f"\nFIPS range check (should be 1000-79999): {fips_valid.all()}")
print(f"FIPS min: {df['fips'].min()}, max: {df['fips'].max()}")

# Check for 0 or missing FIPS patterns
print(f"\nRows with FIPS=0 or missing:")
print(df[df['fips'].isna() | (df['fips'] == 0)].head(10))

Purpose: Analyze county/state relationships and deaths null patterns
Code:
# Check county/state combinations - this might explain the high unique counts
print("County/State combination analysis:")
county_state_counts = df.groupby(['county', 'state']).size()
print(f"Unique county-state combinations: {len(county_state_counts)}")

# Check how many times each county appears across states
county_counts_by_state = df.groupby('county')['state'].nunique()
multi_state_counties = county_counts_by_state[county_counts_by_state > 1].sort_values(ascending=False)
print(f"\nCounties appearing in multiple states: {len(multi_state_counties)}")
print("Top multi-state counties:")
print(multi_state_counts_by_state.head(20))

# Check the deaths column nulls
print(f"\n\nDeaths analysis:")
print(f"Deaths null count: {df['deaths'].isna().sum()}")
print(f"Deaths zero count: {(df['deaths'] == 0).sum()}")
print(f"Deaths row percentage null: {df['deaths'].isna().sum() / len(df) * 100:.2f}%")

# Comprehensive analysis of the actual dataset
print("=== DATASET STRUCTURE ===")
print(f"Shape: {df.shape}")
print(f"\nColumn types:")
print(df.dtypes)

print("\n\n=== MISSING VALUES ===")
for col in df.columns:
    print(f"{col}: {df[col].isna().sum()} nulls ({df[col].isna().sum()/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")

print("\n\n=== NUMERIC SUMMARY ===")
print(df[['cases', 'deaths', 'fips']].describe())

print("\n\n=== DATE ANALYSIS ===")
df_dates = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
print(f"Date range: {df_dates.min().date()} to {df_dates.max().date()}")
print(f"Records per date distribution:")
print(df['date'].value_counts().describe())

Purpose: Analyze state distribution and cases/deaths relationship
Code:
# State distribution and case/death analysis
print("=== STATE DISTRIBUTION ===")
print(f"Unique states: {df['state'].nunique()}")
print(df['state'].value_counts())

print("\n\n=== CASES VS DEATHS ANALYSIS ===")
# Check relationship between cases and deaths
import numpy as np
valid_data = df.dropna(subset=['cases', 'deaths'])
correlation = np.corrcoef(valid_data['cases'], valid_data['deaths'])[0,1]
print(f"Correlation between cases and deaths: {correlation:.4f}")

# Death rate analysis
df['death_rate'] = df['deaths'] / df['cases'] * 100
print(f"\nDeath rate (deaths/cases %) - mean: {df['death_rate'].mean():.2f}%")
print(f"Death rate - median: {df['death_rate'].median():.2f}%")

print("\n\nTop records by cases:")
print(df.nlargest(10, 'cases')[['county', 'state', 'cases', 'deaths', 'death_rate']])

print("\n\nRecords with highest death rates (min 100 cases):")
high_case = df[df['cases'] >= 100].nlargest(10, 'death_rate')[['county', 'state', 'cases', 'deaths', 'death_rate']]

Purpose: Validate FIPS codes for data quality
Code:
# Check FIPS format and validity
print("=== FIPS VALIDATION ===")
fips_clean = df['fips'].dropna()

# Valid FIPS codes: state (2 digits) + county (3 digits) = 5 digits
# Some might be missing leading zeros
print(f"FIPS values with decimal: {fips_clean.apply(lambda x: len(str(x).split('.')[-1]) > 0).sum()}")

# Check if values have correct format when converted to int
fips_int = fips_clean.astype(int)
print(f"FIPS min: {fips_int.min()}, max: {fips_int.max()}")

# FIPS 00xx codes are often for special areas (boroughs, independent cities, etc.)
# FIPS 1000+ are valid county codes
# Check for unusual FIPS codes
print(f"\nFIPS values < 1000: {len(fips_int[fips_int < 1000])}")
print(f"FIPS values < 1000 sample: {fips_int[fips_int < 1000].head(10).tolist()}")

# Check for FIPS values that are too large
print(f"\nFIPS values > 70000 (should be rare): {len(fips_int[fips_int > 70000])}")
print(f"FIPS > 70000 samples: {fips_int[fips_int > 70000].head(10).tolist()}")

# Look up the county for FIPS 1071
print(f"\nRows with FIPS 1071:")
print(df[df['fips'] == 1071.0])
Output:

1. Understand date range and frequency of data collection

Tool: run_code

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# First, let's understand the date range and structure
print("Date range:")
print(f"Min date: {df['date'].min()}")
print(f"Max date: {df['date'].max()}")
print(f"\nDate distribution sample:")
date_counts = df['date'].value_counts()
print(f"Min records per date: {date_counts.min()}")
print(f"Max records per date: {date_counts.max()}")
print(f"Mean records per date: {date_counts.mean():.0f}")

# Check date frequency
df_dates = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
print(f"\nDate range (datetime): {df_dates.min()} to {df_dates.max()}")
print(f"Unique dates: {df_dates.nunique()}")
print(f"Days in range: {(df_dates.max() - df_dates.min()).days + 1}")

Tool: run_code

# Check the state column more carefully - the values seem unusual
print("State distribution:")
print(f"Total unique states: {df['state'].nunique()}")
print(f"All states in dataset:")
states = df['state'].unique()
states_sorted = sorted(states)
print(states_sorted)

# Verify these are actual US states/territories
us_states = ['Alabama', 'Alaska', 'Arizona', 'Arkansas', 'California', 'Colorado', 
             'Connecticut', 'Delaware', 'Florida', 'Georgia', 'Hawaii', 'Idaho', 
             'Illinois', 'Indiana', 'Iowa', 'Kansas', 'Kentucky', 'Louisiana', 
             'Maine', 'Maryland', 'Massachusetts', 'Michigan', 'Minnesota', 
             'Mississippi', 'Missouri', 'Montana', 'Nebraska', 'Nevada',

             'Wyoming', 'District of Columbia', 'Puerto Rico', 'Guam', 'Northern Mariana Islands',

4. Examine FIPS values for validity

Tool: run_code

# Re-examine FIPS - the nulls should be checked more carefully
print("FIPS analysis:")
print(f"FIPS dtype: {df['fips'].dtype}")
print(f"FIPS null count: {df['fips'].isna().sum()}")
print(f"FIPS sample values:")
print(df['fips'].dropna().head(20).tolist())

# Check if FIPS values look valid (should be 5-digit numbers)
fips_valid = df['fips'].dropna().apply(lambda x: 1000 <= x <= 79999)
print(f"\nFIPS range check (should be 1000-79999): {fips_valid.all()}")
print(f"FIPS min: {df['fips'].min()}, max: {df['fips'].max()}")

# Check for 0 or missing FIPS patterns
print(f"\nRows with FIPS=0 or missing:")
print(df[df['fips'].isna() | (df['fips'] == 0)].head(10))

5. Analyze county/state relationships and deaths null patterns

Tool: run_code

# Check county/state combinations - this might explain the high unique counts
print("County/State combination analysis:")
county_state_counts = df.groupby(['county', 'state']).size()
print(f"Unique county-state combinations: {len(county_state_counts)}")

# Check how many times each county appears across states
county_counts_by_state = df.groupby('county')['state'].nunique()
multi_state_counties = county_counts_by_state[county_counts_by_state > 1].sort_values(ascending=False)
print(f"\nCounties appearing in multiple states: {len(multi_state_counties)}")
print("Top multi-state counties:")
print(multi_state_counts_by_state.head(20))

# Check the deaths column nulls
print(f"\n\nDeaths analysis:")
print(f"Deaths null count: {df['deaths'].isna().sum()}")
print(f"Deaths zero count: {(df['deaths'] == 0).sum()}")
print(f"Deaths row percentage null: {df['deaths'].isna().sum() / len(df) * 100:.2f}%")

10. Analyze state distribution and cases/deaths relationship

Tool: run_code

# State distribution and case/death analysis
print("=== STATE DISTRIBUTION ===")
print(f"Unique states: {df['state'].nunique()}")
print(df['state'].value_counts())

print("\n\n=== CASES VS DEATHS ANALYSIS ===")
# Check relationship between cases and deaths
import numpy as np
valid_data = df.dropna(subset=['cases', 'deaths'])
correlation = np.corrcoef(valid_data['cases'], valid_data['deaths'])[0,1]
print(f"Correlation between cases and deaths: {correlation:.4f}")

# Death rate analysis
df['death_rate'] = df['deaths'] / df['cases'] * 100
print(f"\nDeath rate (deaths/cases %) - mean: {df['death_rate'].mean():.2f}%")
print(f"Death rate - median: {df['death_rate'].median():.2f}%")

print("\n\nTop records by cases:")
print(df.nlargest(10, 'cases')[['county', 'state', 'cases', 'deaths', 'death_rate']])

print("\n\nRecords with highest death rates (min 100 cases):")
high_case = df[df['cases'] >= 100].nlargest(10, 'death_rate')[['county', 'state', 'cases', 'deaths', 'death_rate']]

keyboard_arrow_down

11. Validate FIPS codes for data quality

Tool: run_code

# Check FIPS format and validity
print("=== FIPS VALIDATION ===")
fips_clean = df['fips'].dropna()

# Valid FIPS codes: state (2 digits) + county (3 digits) = 5 digits
# Some might be missing leading zeros
print(f"FIPS values with decimal: {fips_clean.apply(lambda x: len(str(x).split('.')[-1]) > 0).sum()}")

# Check if values have correct format when converted to int
fips_int = fips_clean.astype(int)
print(f"FIPS min: {fips_int.min()}, max: {fips_int.max()}")

# FIPS 00xx codes are often for special areas (boroughs, independent cities, etc.)
# FIPS 1000+ are valid county codes
# Check for unusual FIPS codes
print(f"\nFIPS values < 1000: {len(fips_int[fips_int < 1000])}")
print(f"FIPS values < 1000 sample: {fips_int[fips_int < 1000].head(10).tolist()}")

# Check for FIPS values that are too large
print(f"\nFIPS values > 70000 (should be rare): {len(fips_int[fips_int > 70000])}")
print(f"FIPS > 70000 samples: {fips_int[fips_int > 70000].head(10).tolist()}")

# Look up the county for FIPS 1071
print(f"\nRows with FIPS 1071:")
print(df[df['fips'] == 1071.0])

Output

FIPS min: 1071, max: 72149

FIPS values < 1000: 0
FIPS values < 1000 sample: []

FIPS > 70000 samples: [72065, 72057, 72041, 72149, 72125, 72013]

# Check for duplicate county-state-date combinations
